options.why: Different variables ($m$ and $n$); a matching layout is mistaken for like terms.
Different powers ($m$ and $m^2$); the matching coefficient $4$ is mistaken for like terms.
One term has variable $m$, the other is a constant, so they are unlike.
Both are $z^2$; different coefficients are wrongly read as making them unlike.
Same variable and power; the coefficient difference is mistaken for unlike.
Sign difference mistaken for unlike; both have variable part $z^2$.

options.text: Yes — same variables $a$ and $y$, and order does not matter.
No — the letters are written in a different order.
options.why: Believes variable order matters; $ay$ and $ya$ are the same product.

options.text: No — the coefficients $4$ and $5$ are different.
options.why: Decides by the coefficients; coefficients do not affect whether terms are like.
Imagines a hidden power difference; both terms are $a^1y^1$.
The square is on a different letter; $a^2y$ and $ay^2$ have their powers swapped.
The second term drops the $y$; a missing variable makes them unlike.
Extra power on $y$; $y^1$ and $y^2$ differ, so the terms are unlike.
A constant and a variable term are unlike; the matching $8$ is a coefficient, not a like part.
One has variable $x$, the other is a constant, so they are unlike.
A plain number and a variable are not like terms.

questions.q: Which terms in $7f + 7g - 3f + 3$ are like terms?

options.why: Matched the coefficient $7$ instead of the variable; $f$ and $g$ are different.
Included $7g$, which has variable $g$, not $f$.
Paired a variable term with the constant $3$; a constant is not like $-3f$.

questions.q: Which term is **unlike** the other two: $xy^2$, $x^2y$, $y^2x$?

options.why: $xy^2$ and $y^2x$ are both $x^1y^2$; the odd one out is $x^2y$, not $xy^2$.
$y^2x$ reorders to $xy^2$, so it is like $xy^2$; the odd one is $x^2y$.

questions.q: In $2pq + 3qp + 9p + 9q$, which are the like terms?

options.why: Grouped every term with a $p$ or $q$; $9p$ and $9q$ each have only one variable.
Matched the coefficient $9$; $p$ and $q$ are different variables.
Added $9p$, which lacks the $q$, so it is unlike $pq$.
